explanation: The natural language statement describes a property that must hold true at all times: if a certain condition (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35) is met, then another condition (RWs_torque_equalTo_0) must also be true. The 'If A, then B' structure translates directly to a logical implication (A -> B). Since this relationship must continuously hold, the global operator 'G' is used to assert that this implication is always true.

The natural language statement describes a global implication: at any point in time, if the condition 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is met, then the condition 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also be true simultaneously. The 'G' (Globally) operator ensures that this implication holds true at all times in the system's execution. The '->' (implies) operator correctly captures the 'if...then...' relationship.

The natural language statement "If A, then B" translates to the logical implication A -> B. The phrase "shall be" indicates that this condition must always hold, meaning it's a global invariant. Therefore, if 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is true at any point, then 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also be true at that same point. The 'G' operator ensures this implication holds globally throughout the system's execution.

The statement "If P, then Q" translates to the implication "P -> Q". The phrase "shall be equal to" implies that this condition must always hold throughout the system's execution. Therefore, the global operator 'G' is used to assert that the implication `RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0` must always be true.

The natural language statement specifies a condition that must always hold: if 'RWs_angular_momentum' is greater than 0.35, then 'RWs_torque' must be equal to 0 at that same time. The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this rule is applied at every moment in time. The '->' (implication) operator captures the 'if-then' relationship directly. 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is the antecedent, and 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' is the consequent, meaning whenever the former is true, the latter must also be true.

The natural language statement describes a global implication: if a certain condition (angular momentum greater than 0.35) is met at any point in time, then another condition (torque equal to 0) must also be true at that same point. The LTL operator 'G' (globally) ensures that this implication holds for all future states. The '->' (implication) operator captures the 'If P then Q' structure, where 'P' is 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' and 'Q' is 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0'. Therefore, the LTL formula 'G (RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0)' accurately translates the requirement.

The statement "If the value of signal RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then the value of signal RWs_torque shall be equal to 0." indicates a continuous implication that must hold at all times. The 'If A, then B' structure translates to A -> B. The 'shall be' implies that this condition must always be true. Therefore, we use the global operator (G) to assert that the implication 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 implies RWs_torque_equalTo_0' holds at every moment.

The natural language statement specifies a conditional relationship that must hold at all times: if a certain condition (RWs_angular_momentum being greater than 0.35) is met, then another condition (RWs_torque being equal to 0) must also be true. This 'if A then B' relationship, when applied globally, is best represented by a global implication. The 'G' operator ensures that this implication holds true at every state in the system's execution. Therefore, 'G(RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35 -> RWs_torque_equalTo_0)' precisely captures that it is always the case that if RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35, then RWs_torque is equal to 0.

The natural language statement specifies a condition that must always hold: if 'RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35' is true at any point in time, then 'RWs_torque_equalTo_0' must also be true at that same point in time. The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this implication holds throughout the entire execution. The '->' (implication) operator correctly captures the 'If... then...' relationship between the two atomic propositions.

The natural language statement "If A, then B" translates to a global implication `G(A -> B)`. In this case, "A" is "RWs_angular_momentum is greater than 0.35" (represented by `RWs_angular_momentum_greaterThan_0.35`) and "B" is "RWs_torque shall be equal to 0" (represented by `RWs_torque_equalTo_0`). The "shall be" indicates that this condition must hold true whenever the premise is met, at all times, hence the `G` operator for 'globally'.
